coredata.dc:title: Zinc-doped carbon quantum dots-based ratiometric fluorescence probe for rapid, specific, and visual determination of tetracycline hydrochloride
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Feijiao
Fu, Quanbin
Li, Yijing
Yan, Cuijuan
Xiao, Dehui
Ju, Peng
Hu, Zhiyong
Li, Houshen
Ai, Shiyun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a rapid, specific, and visual ratiometric fluorescence probe was constructed for tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) determination based on zinc-doped carbon quantum dots (Zn-CDs). In the presence of TCH, the blue fluorescence at 440 nm originating from Zn-CDs was quenched, and the green fluorescence at 515 nm stemming from TCH was enhanced. The inner filter effect (IFE) and the chelation between Zn and tetracycline are the main mechanisms for the conversion of spectra. The spectrum and color change completed and stabilized within 1 min, indicating the possibility of real-time detection of TCH. The detection range for TCH is 0.1–50 μM, and the low detection limit is 61.1 nM. In addition, Zn-CDs-based test strips were successfully applied to direct visual identification of TCH in actual samples of river water and milk, indicating the possibility of their practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Carbon quantum dots
Tetracyclines
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Zn-CDs ratiometric fluorescence detection of TCs
Recovery rate experiment in a real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Zn-CDs: (a) TEM image (inset: HRTEM image), (b) size distribution histogram, (c) FTIR spectra, (d) XRD pattern, and (e) XPS survey spectrum. The corresponding high-resolution spectra of (f) C 1 s, (g) O 1 s, and (h) Zn 2p.
Schematic illustration of (a) Zn-CDs preparation and (b) ratiometric fluorescence detection of TCH.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of Zn-CDs with different concentrations of TCH. (b) The linear relationship between I515/I440 and the TCH concentration. (c) CIE 1931 chromaticity. (d) Photographs of Zn-CDs solutions with various TCH concentrations under 365 nm UV light.

Fluorescence spectra, selectivity, and fluorescence photos of Zn-CDs probes with TCs, various ions, and molecules. The concentrations of TCs, ions, and molecules were 25 μM, 200 μM, and 200 μM, respectively.

(a) UV–vis spectrum of TCH and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Zn-CDs. (b) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of Zn-CDs in the absence and presence of TCH. (c) Stern-Volmer plot under various temperatures from 288 K to 308 K. (d) UV–vis spectra of CDs, CDs-TCH, Zn-CDs, Zn-CDs-TCH, and TCH.

head.ce:title: Zinc-doped carbon quantum dots-based ratiometric fluorescence probe for rapid, specific, and visual determination of tetracycline hydrochloride

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: contribute equal to this work as first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a rapid, specific, and visual ratiometric fluorescence probe was constructed for tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) determination based on zinc-doped carbon quantum dots (Zn-CDs). In the presence of TCH, the blue fluorescence at 440 nm originating from Zn-CDs was quenched, and the green fluorescence at 515 nm stemming from TCH was enhanced. The inner filter effect (IFE) and the chelation between Zn and tetracycline are the main mechanisms for the conversion of spectra. The spectrum and color change completed and stabilized within 1 min, indicating the possibility of real-time detection of TCH. The detection range for TCH is 0.1–50 μM, and the low detection limit is 61.1 nM. In addition, Zn-CDs-based test strips were successfully applied to direct visual identification of TCH in actual samples of river water and milk, indicating the possibility of their practical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs), including tetracycline (TC), chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC) and so on, are a class of broad-spectrum antibiotics produced by actinomycetes. As a developmental feed additive, TCs have been widely used in animal husbandry, and tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) has become ubiquitous. However, the overuse of TCs can lead to the contamination of food and environmental water, both of which pose a threat to human health. Many countries have implemented strict regulations on the maximum residue limits (MRLs) of TCs in food to ensure food safety (Gan, Zhang, et al., 2021). For instance, the MRL of TCs in milk is 100 ng mL−1. Therefore, the development of fast and accurate analysis techniques for TCs is a focus of research.

To date, many well-established analytical methods have been constructed for the qualitative and quantitative detection of TCs, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Pang, Lv, Sun, Yang, & Shen, 2021; Wang, Lin, Huang, & Chen, 2017), electrochemical analysis (Dong, Wen, Du, Li, & Li, 2022; Zhong, Wang, Piao, & Chen, 2021), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (Muhammad, Yan, Yao, Chao, Zhu, & Huang, 2020; Zhao, Wang, Chen, Wang, Yao, & Wang, 2023), and so forth. Analytical methods utilizing large instruments can obtain accurate analysis results; these methods are suitable for confirmation of results rather than on-site acquisition analysis. Fluorescence (FL) sensors are considered an ideal method for the on-site detection of TCs due to their ease of operation, visualization, and sensitivity (Gan, Hu, et al., 2021; Han, yang, et al., 2020; Jia et al., 2021). The single-emitted fluorescence probe is susceptible to interference from the detection environment (Wang, Li, et al., 2022). Ratiometric fluorescence probes establish a self-calibration system based on the intensity relationship of emission peaks at two wavelengths, which can solve background interference. In addition, the apparent fluorescence color transition of ratiometric fluorescence sensors is easier to identify, which is beneficial for the construction of smartphone-assisted sensing and paper-based fluorescence sensors (Han, Fan, et al., 2020).

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are gaining popularity in the field of TC detection owing to their low toxicity, good optical properties, high biocompatibility, and easy surface modification (Miao et al., 2023; Wareing, Gentile, & Phan, 2021). However, CDs exhibit lower fluorescence intensity than traditional semiconductor quantum dots. Recently, the method of heteroatom doping CDs has become useful for improving fluorescence properties, and various heteroatom doping methods have been reported (Kou, Jiang, Park, & Meng, 2020; Wang, Sun, Yang, Liu, Xue, & Fan, 2022). Most reports focus on the doping of nonmetallic atoms, such as N, S or B (Li, Yang, & Xu, 2019). B, N-codoped CDs with bright blue fluorescence, which can achieve sensitive detection of TCs by reducing fluorescence intensity with the internal filtering effect of TC, have been synthesized (Hu et al., 2022). Furthermore, some metal atoms, such as Cu and Zn, are used as dopants to expand the application of CDs (Cheng, Wang, Zhang, Yang, & Chen, 2016; Guo et al., 2021; Yao, Li, Huo, Gong, Li, et al., 2022). Huang successfully synthesized Zn-doped CDs and accurately analyzed the antioxidant activity index of multiple samples by Fenton chemistry, which can overcome the problems of complex samples, photobleaching and low stability in traditional technology (Xu et al., 2018). Research has also found that the interaction between zinc and TC can enhance the green fluorescence intensity, thereby achieving sensitive detection of TC. Yao, Li, Huo, Gong, Liu, et al. (2022) successfully used l-histidine-functionalized zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) with aggregation-induced emission for the detection of TC. Yang (Yang, Jia, Hou, Zhao, & Wang, 2022) used Ru@ZIF-8 as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the sensitive and visual detection of TCs. In the presence of TC, the red fluorescence of Ru(bpy)3 2+ is not affected, while the green fluorescence originating from the interaction between ZIF-8 and TCs is enhanced. However, the design of ratiometric fluorescence sensors with zinc-doped CDs for the sensitive and fast detection of TCs is still a challenge.

Herein, a fluorescence sensor was developed for fast and sensitive detection of TCH using zinc-doped carbon quantum dots (Zn-CDs) as the fluorescent probe. Zn-CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using sodium citrate and zinc chloride as precursors. The prepared Zn-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence performance compared with pure CDs. The bright blue fluorescence of Zn-CDs can be quenched by TCH due to the inner filter effect (IFE). Furthermore, TCH can chelate with Zn2+, accompanied by enhancement of green fluorescence. According to the radio value of blue fluorescent intensity at 440 nm (I440) and green fluorescent intensity at 515 nm (I515), a ratiometric fluorescent sensor (I515/I440)-based Zn-CDs was constructed for the determination of TCH. The Zn-CDs ratiometric fluorescent sensor exhibits excellent sensitivity, selectivity, and ultrafast response. In addition, the smart semiquantitative sensing performance and paper-based sensing performance of the sensor were verified. The practicality of this method was tested by the determination of TCH in tap water, river water, milk, eggs, and chicken.

Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane, lysine (Lys), tryptophan (Try), alanine (Ala), arginine (Arg), tyrosine (Tyr), glutamatergic (Glu), phytate (IP6), trisodium citrate dihydrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O), CH3COONa, FeCl3·H2O, NaCl, NaH2PO4·2H2O, Na2HPO4·12H2O, and NaOH were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Tetracycline (TC), tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH), oxytetracycline (OTC), cysteine (Cys), chloramphenicol (CTC), l-aspartic acid (Asp), histidine (His), vitamin B1 (VB1), glutathione, Mg(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, and KCl were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (NH4)2SO4, Pb(CH3COO)2, MnCl2, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Ca(NO3)2, Na2SO4, Na2SO3, NaNO3, NaNO2, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, NaBr, hydrochloric acid, and acetate were purchased from Tianjin Kaitong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The above chemical reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without extra purification.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker IR spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained by a JEOL JEM-2100F. The crystalline structure of Zn-CDs was characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker AXS D8 Advance). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained by a Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer. Fluorescence (FL) spectra and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded from a Cary Eclipse spectrometer and a Shimadzu UV–vis spectrometer. Fluorescence lifetime was measured using an Edinburgh FLS980 spectrometer. Quantum yield (QY) and excitation-emission-matrix spectra (EEMS) were characterized by a SHIMADZU RF6000 spectrometer. The QY of Zn-CDs was calculated by the quinine sulfate method (details shown in the Supplementary material). All fluorescence optical images were captured at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm with an ultraviolet lamp.

The Zn-CDs were synthesized through a facile hydrothermal method with citrate and ZnCl2 as precursors (Sohail, Xie, Li, & Huang, 2022). In detail, 0.88 g trisodium citrate dihydrate was dissolved in 30 mL ultrapure water, and then 0.2 g ZnCl2 was subsequently added. The mixture was transferred to an autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 7 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the aqueous solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter. The filtrate was dialyzed in a 100 Da dialysis bag for 12 h to remove excess raw material. Finally, the product was collected by a rotary evaporation instrument for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zn-CDs ratiometric fluorescence detection of TCs
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 0.3 mL of Zn-CDs solution (2 mg mL−1) was mixed with 0.4 mL of Tris-HCl solution (0.05 M, pH = 7), 0.2 mL of TCH solution with different concentrations was subsequently added, and the final solution volume was maintained at 2 mL with ultrapure water. After a 5 min reaction at room temperature, the FL spectra of the mixtures were recorded under 360 nm excitation. To ensure the accuracy of the experimental results, parallel measurements were conducted three times.

TCH was replaced by other antibiotics (TC, OTC, and CTC), molecules (GSH, His, Lys, Cys, Ala, Arg, Trp, Tyr, Asp, Glu, IP6, and VB1), anions (SO4 2-, SO3 2-, Cl-, NO3 –, NO2 –, HCO3 –, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 -, Br-, Ac-, CO3 2–, and S2-), and cations (Co2+, Na+, NH4 +, Pb2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, and Fe3+) to verify the selectivity of the Zn-CDs probe for TCH. The molecule and ion concentrations were 0.2 mM instead of 25 μM TCH. The anti-interference property of Zn-CDs for TCH was investigated by mixing TCH (25 μM) and the others (0.2 mM).

The RGB values of the solution fluorescence colors under 365 nm excitation were analyzed in real time with the smartphone color recognition application. The G/B values were used to accurately determine the TCH concentration.

The test papers were prepared for convenient TCH detection. Filter papers were dripped with 200 μL of Zn-CDs solution (20 mg/mL) and dried under natural conditions. The dried test papers were used to detect TCH in river water and raw milk.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery rate experiment in a real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The tap water and river water samples were processed according to reported methods before measurement (Yao, Li, Liu, Fan, & Pu, 2021). The milk, egg and chicken samples were treated according to the China national standard method (GB31658.17–2021). The details are shown in the Supplementary material. The accuracy of the established method was compared with the China national standard method (GB31658.17–2021) for detecting tetracyclines by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC).

Zn CDs with blue fluorescence were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method using citrate and ZnCl2 as precursors. Fig. 1 shows the characterization of Zn-CDs for their morphology, size, crystal structure, and functional groups. The TEM image (Fig. 1a) shows that the prepared Zn-CDs had uniform dimensions, and the average diameter was approximately 1.9 ± 0.8 nm (Fig. 1b), which indicates good dispersibility of Zn-CDs. As shown in the HRTEM image (inset in Fig. 1a), the lattice distance of Zn-CDs is approximately 0.225 nm (Xu et al., 2018). The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1c) displayed the functional groups of Zn-CDs. Both OH bending vibration (1249 cm−1) and stretching vibration (3461 cm−1) were observed. Additionally, the peaks at 1587 cm−1, 1403 cm−1, and 1095 cm−1 were attributed to the CO stretching vibrations or COO– asymmetric vibrations, CH bending and stretching vibrations or COO– symmetric vibrations, and CC/O stretching vibrations, respectively (Sohail, Xie, Li, & Huang, 2022). Furthermore, the characteristic peaks at 450 cm−1 were assigned to the Zn-O vibration, which implied the formation of complexes between oxygen and zinc (Cheng, Wang, Zhang, Yang, & Chen, 2016; Das et al., 2018). The XRD pattern of Zn-CDs (Fig. 1d) showed a broad diffraction peak (20°–40°), which revealed its amorphous nature (Hou, Yan, Zhao, Li, Ding, & Ding, 2013; Yun, Kang, & Choi, 2022).

The elemental compositions and chemical bonds of Zn-CDs were investigated by XPS (Fig. 1e-1 h). The peaks at 284.07 eV, 529.26 eV, and 1021.98 eV found in the XPS survey spectrum were ascribed to C 1 s, O 1 s, and Zn 2p, respectively (Fig. 1e). The XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 1f) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.59 eV, 285.56 eV, and 288.41 eV, which contributed to CC/CC, CO, and CO, respectively. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1g) revealed three peaks at 531.43 eV, 532.47 eV, and 535.53 eV, highlighting Zn-O, CO, and CO, respectively. For Zn 2p, as exhibited in Fig. 2 h, two peaks were fitted at 1045.18 eV and 1021.98 eV. Consequently, the results confirmed the Zn doping of CDs and the successful preparation of Zn-CDs (Cheng, Wang, Zhang, Yang, & Chen, 2016; He et al., 2021). Moreover, the QY of the Zn-CDs was calculated to be 26.84%.

The basic design principle of Zn-CDs for TCH detection is presented in Fig. 2. The prepared Zn-CDs solid powder exhibited a clear bright blue color under UV light (365 nm excitation). After dissolving in water, the Zn-CDs solution (0.3 mg/mL) still displayed blue fluorescence at 440 nm under 365 nm excitation. Additionally, CDs without Zn doping were synthesized using only citrate as the carbon source. As shown in Fig. S1, CDs (0.3 mg/mL) have almost no fluorescence. These results demonstrated the essentiality of Zn in the preparation of fluorescent CDs.

Zn-CDs were used as ratiometric fluorescence probes for TCH detection. TCH has weak fluorescence at 515 nm on its own, but the faint intensity is not sufficient for TCH detection. When TCH was mixed with Zn-CDs, the blue fluorescence intensity (I440) of Zn-CDs was gradually quenched, while the green fluorescence intensity (I515) significantly emerged, and the fluorescence color changed from blue to green; this color change can easily realize TCH determination. The same procedure was performed using CDs. However, there was no obvious fluorescence color change (Fig. S1). The same conclusion can be drawn from the excitation − emission-matrix spectra of CDs, CDs-TCH, Zn-CDs, Zn-CDs-TCH, and TCH (Fig. S2). Only Zn-CDs and Zn-CDs-TCH displayed obvious and distinct fluorescence signals. Therefore, Zn doping is also crucial for ratiometric fluorescence TCH sensing.

The detection conditions, including excitation wavelength, solution pH, and reaction time, were studied to achieve the optimal analytical results. The fluorescence emission spectra of Zn-CDs with TCH (Zn-CDs-TCH) under different excitation wavelengths (330–370 nm) were compared to determine the appropriate excitation wavelengths (Fig. S3). Zn-CDs-TCH exhibited an excitation-dependent fluorescence feature. As shown in Fig. S3 a, the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm (I440) reached a maximum at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm and then gradually decreased. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity at 515 nm (I515) steadily increased with increasing excitation wavelength. To determine the transformation of the emission spectra more directly, the ratio of the fluorescence intensity at 515 nm and 440 nm (I515/I440) was calculated (Fig. S3b). The value of I515/I440 increased with increasing excitation wavelength. To provide a suitable initial blue fluorescence intensity for ratiometric fluorescence detection, 360 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength in further studies.

The pH values of the surroundings are always an important factor for target detection, affecting the sensitivity and accuracy of the results. The effect of pH values was investigated by adjusting the pH with HCl or NaOH. As shown in Fig. S4, when the solution pH values changed from 4 to 9, there was no significant change in the blue fluorescence intensity of Zn-CDs at 440 nm. Green fluorescence at 515 nm emerged after adding TCH to Zn-CDs. When the pH value increased from 4 to 9, I515 first increased and then decreased, reaching a maximum at a pH value of 7. Furthermore, the ratio of I515/I440 was the highest at pH 7 (Fig. S5). Therefore, pH 7 was applied in further tests.

The incubation time of the Zn-CDs-TCH system was examined by monitoring the fluorescence intensity ratio (Fig. S6). When TCH was added into the Zn-CDs solution, the ratio of I515/I440 increased rapidly and significantly within 1 min and tended to stabilize. The results indicated that the Zn-CDs probe has an ultrasensitive and ultrarapid response to TCH, and the ultrahigh stability for TCH detection ensures the accuracy of the results. To ensure the consistency of the measurement, 10 min was selected as the incubation time. Overall, the detection condition parameters were chosen as follows: excitation wavelength at 360 nm, pH value of 7, and incubation time of 10 min.

The fluorescence spectra of Zn-CDs aqueous solution with varying concentrations of TCH were tested to verify the potential of Zn-CDs as a fluorescence probe for TCH detection (Fig. 3a). As shown in Fig. 3a, when the concentration of TCH increased, the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm (I440), originating from Zn-CDs, gradually decreased, while the fluorescence intensity at 515 nm (I515), stemming from the combination of TCH and Zn-CDs, gradually increased. However, when the concentration of TCH was higher than 60 μM, I515 began to decline. A reasonable explanation for this phenomenon was that a saturation point was present, and the Zn-TCH complex could not form infinitely with increasing TCH (Li, Zhu, Xie, Ren, & Zheng, 2018). As shown in Fig. 3b, the value of I515/I440 gradually increased, and there was a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.986) between the value of I515/I440 and the concentration of TCH in the range of 0.1 to 50 μM, giving a fitting equation of I515/I440 = 0.05116C TCH + 0.30009. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 61.1 nM according to Eq. S1.

In addition, the fluorescence emission of the solution changed obviously from light blue to green under a UV lamp with 365 nm excitation, which is consistent with the 1931 CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. 3c and d). The results indicated that the Zn-CDs ratiometric fluorescence probe could realize the quantitative and visual detection of TCH. In addition, the proposed method exhibited comparable high sensitivity and a wide detection range compared with previous works on CD detection of TCH, as summarized in Table S1.

Various ions or molecules may also cause changes in fluorescence signals. Therefore, the selectivity of the ratio fluorescence Zn-CDs probe for TCH detection was evaluated. Various cations (Co2+, Na+, NH4 +, Pb2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, and Fe3+), anions (SO4 2-, SO3 2-, Cl-, NO3 –, NO2 –, HCO3 –, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 -, Br-, Ac-, CO3 2–, and S2-), molecules (GSH, His, Lys, Cys, Ala, Arg, Trp, Tyr, Asp, Glu, IP6, and VB1), and TCs (TCH, TC, OTC, and CTC) were added to the Zn-CDs solution, and the variation response was measured. The concentrations of the other substances and TCs were 200 μM and 25 μM, respectively. As presented in Fig. 4 , compared with the blank, only TCH, OTC, TC, or CTC allowed the fluorescence spectra of Zn-CDs to exhibit distinct changes, while the value of I515/I440 increased significantly. When other cations, anions, or molecules were mixed with Zn-CDs, I515/I440 showed a faint difference, and the fluorescence color of the solution remained blue under UV light. These results confirmed the specificity and universality of the Zn-CDs probe for TCs detection.

The coexistence of other disruptors and TCH may affect the authenticity of the test results. Various ions or molecules may affect the fluorescence property of Zn-CDs or the interaction between Zn-CDs and TCs. Therefore, anti-interference experiments of the Zn-CDs probe for TCH detection were carried out in the presence of other substances, including various cations (Co2+, Na+, NH4 +, Pb2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, and Fe3+), anions (SO4 2-, SO3 2-, Cl-, NO3 –, NO2 –, HCO3 –, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 -, Br-, Ac-, CO3 2–, and S2-), and molecules (GSH, His, Lys, Cys, Ala, Arg, Trp, Tyr, Asp, Glu, IP6, and VB1). The concentrations of TCH and other disruptors were 25 μM and 200 μM, respectively. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence spectra and I515/I440 values did not significantly change for general coexisting substances. Somewhat differently, when Ca2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ were present (200 μM), the I515/I440 value decreased slightly in sequence. When Cu2+ was present, the fluorescence intensities at 440 nm and 515 nm were both partly quenched, and the I515/I440 values decreased significantly due to chelation between TCH and Cu2+. However, the fluorescence color of the solutions still transformed to green under UV light irradiation. The interference of influential cations could be ignored owing to their low content in most environmental and food testing scenarios. The results showed that the Zn-CDs ratiometric fluorescence probe has high anti-interference ability for TCH detection.

It is worth noting that Al3+ enhanced the fluorescence of TCH. Therefore, the effect of the Al3+ concentration on the fluorescence spectra and I515/I440 value of the system was also studied (Fig. S8). Interestingly, the emission wavelength at 515 nm of Zn-CDs-TCH underwent a blueshift, and I515 increased as the Al3+ concentration increased. Such a blueshift phenomenon may be caused by the synergism of Al3+ and Zn2+ with TCH (Qu, Sun, Liu, Zhao, & You, 2016). Moreover, a good linear relationship was observed between the I515/I440 ratio and lg[C Al] (the concentration of Al3+ ranged from 0.5 μM to 100 μM). This phenomenon indicates that the sensing system has potential for Al3+ detection.

IFE refers to the overlap between the excitation or emission fluorescence spectra of the fluorophore and the absorbance spectrum of the quenching agent. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence excitation spectrum of Zn-CDs overlapped with the absorbance spectrum of TCH. The results suggested that the quenching of blue fluorescence of Zn-CDs-TCH is related to the IFE of TCH toward Zn-CDs. In addition, the average fluorescence lifetimes of Zn-CDs with and without TCH were measured (Fig. 5b). The fluorescence lifetimes of Zn-CDs and Zn-CDs-TCH at 440 nm were 6.51 ns and 6.54 ns, respectively. Before and after adding TCH, the fluorescence lifetimes had nearly no change. The results indicated that the quenching of blue fluorescence at 440 nm belonged to static quenching.

Meanwhile, the Stern-Volmer equation was used to describe the fluorescence quenching:
I 0
I
= 1 +
K
SV
C = 1 +
K q
τ 0
C

where I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity (I440) of Zn CDs before and after TCH addition, respectively; C is the concentration of TCH; and K SV is the quenching constant related to the quenching rate constant (K q) and fluorescence lifetime of Zn-CDs (τ 0) (Yang, Jia, Hou, Bu, et al., 2020).

As shown in Fig. 5c, there is a linear relationship between I0/I and the concentration of TCH (0–50 μM) at different incubation temperatures. The K SV values were 2.14 × 104, 1.38 × 104, and 1.01 × 104 M−1 at 288 K, 298 K, and 308 K, respectively, suggesting that TCH had a high quenching efficiency. The slope of the Stern-Volmer plot gradually decreased as the incubation temperature increased, which indicated that increasing the temperature does not improve the number of collisions between Zn-CDs and quenching agents. K q were also calculated to be 3.29 × 1012, 2.11 × 1012, and 1.55 × 1012 M−1 s−1 at 288 K, 298 K, and 308 K, respectively, which were larger than the possible value for the dynamic quenching effect (1.0 × 1012 M−1 s−1). Both fluorescence lifetime measurements and the influence of temperature on the Stern-Volmer plot of Zn-CDs in the absence and presence of TCH show that the quenching mechanism is static quenching.

In addition, the optical properties of CDs, Zn-CDs, CDs-TCH, Zn-CDs-TCH, and TCH were investigated (Fig. 5d). CDs and Zn-CDs have no obvious absorption peak. The absorption peaks of TCH centered at 274 and 361 nm were attributed to ring chromophores (Leng, Zhao, Wang, & Li, 2013). The UV–vis absorbance peak of TCH remained before and after the addition of CDs. However, when TCH was mixed with Zn-CDs, the absorbance peak of Zn-CDs-TCH at approximately 274 nm and 361 nm was blueshifted and redshifted, respectively. Moreover, as presented in Fig. S9, the binding constant (K) of TCH and Zn-CDs was also calculated according to the Benesi-Hildebrand equation:
1
I -
I 0
=
1
K
I
max
-
I 0
C
+
1
I
max
-
I 0

where I0 , I, and Imax are the fluorescence intensities at 515 nm without TCH, with a certain TCH concentration, and with a saturated TCH concentration, respectively, and C represents the concentration of TCH. The binding constant (K) is calculated up to 6.4 × 104 M−1. The change in the UV–vis absorbance spectrum of Zn-CDs-TCH and the binding constant indicated the interaction between Zn2+ and TCH. The results also suggested that the interaction between Zn2+ and TCH led to the emergence of green fluorescence.

The chromaticity program on a smartphone was used to achieve fluorescent color digitization for semiquantitative analysis (Xu, Zhang, Jia, Bi, & Zhao, 2020). As illustrated in Fig. S10, the line detection ranged between the G/B ratio and lg[C TCH] (the concentration of TCH is 3–90 μM). Additionally, a facile and economical test paper based on Zn-CDs was prepared for TCH detection in river water. When the test paper contacted river water containing different concentrations of TCH, the fluorescence color of the test paper changed from blue to green as the concentration of TCH increased (Fig. S11). The applicability of Zn-CDs test paper was also tested in untreated milk samples. As shown in Fig. S12, when in contact with raw milk containing TCH, the test paper also showed fluorescence color changes, although the sensitivity slightly decreased. These results suggested that test papers have a potential application for in situ and real-time detection of real samples, reducing the required pretreatment of the samples.

Moreover, recovery experiments of the Zn-CDs probe were investigated by the standard addition method. Tap water, river water, milk, chicken, and egg were applied as real samples for TCH detection with certain TCH concentrations (5 and 50 μM). The results of the data obtained are listed in Table S2. The TCH recovery rates in tap water, river water, milk, chicken, and egg for 5 and 50 μM TCH were 103.8% and 97.7%, 100.3% and 95.4%, 103.9% and 105.2%, 92% and 106.8%, and 95.8% and 92%, respectively. The conventional high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method was used to verify the accuracy of the Zn-CDs probe, and the values obtained were close to those acquired by the Zn-CDs probe, indicating the reliability of the Zn-CDs probe in actual sample detection.

In brief, Zn-CDs were used as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for TCH detection. When TCH was present, the blue fluorescence centered at 440 nm was quenched, and green fluorescence at 515 nm emerged. The Zn-CDs probe displayed high sensitivity and specificity for TCH determination in the range of 0.1 μM to 50 μM, with LODs of 61.1 nM. The quenching mechanism, which is static quenching, was mainly due to the IFE, while the enhancement mechanism can be attributed to the chelation of Zn and TCs. In addition, the probe was applied for visual and on-site detection of TCH with smartphones and test papers. The Zn-CDs probe was applied to detect TCH in tap water, river water, and milk, demonstrating great potential for convenient and real-time detection in actual samples.

Feijiao Hu: Methodology, Software, Investigation, Conceptualization, Validation, Writing – original draft. Quanbin Fu: Methodology, Software, Investigation, Conceptualization, Validation. Yijing Li: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Cuijuan Yan: Software, Investigation, Data curation. Dehui Xiao: Investigation, Validation. Peng Ju: Investigation, Validation. Zhiyong Hu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Houshen Li: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Shiyun Ai: Supervision, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017BB064), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21503121) and the National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFD0200702).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137097.
